comments.text: With GPT-5.5 Pro I've got a nice connection of this problem to the inverse arithmetic Kakeya problem and [1097]. This allows to establish a partial result with some lower bounds unconditionally like\[ F_{\lfloor\alpha p_u\rfloor}(p_1,\ldots,p_u)\gg u^{6/11}\qquad(\alpha\geq3) \]Here is the note.

Thanks! Standard check claimed a few minor issues. Importantly, it judged the connection to be not new in substance, pointing to Green-Ruzsa, Proposition 4.1. Perhaps others can confirm/not confirm better than me regarding this assessment.

Thanks, yes; this is exactly (one of) the equivalences of the arithmetic Kakeya conjecture found by Green and Ruzsa. (I really should have noticed this, since I know that paper quite well, but clearly the notation in this case is different enough that this didn't jump out at me at the time.) The Green-Ruzsa paper also cites earlier work of Erdős and Selfridge, which clarifies what [Va99] was referring to; I'll update the site accordingly.
Thanks, I suspected it might be known.
See [Er78, p. 35] and [Er86c, p. 60] for more information on the results of Erdős and Selfridge that are being alluded to. Also, compare with #650.

Is this asking for the maximum number of integers, $s$, such that in every interval of $k$ positive integers, there are $s$ integers that are divisible by at least one of $p_1,\ldots,p_u$, OR Is it asking for the maximum number of integers, $s$, such that for every interval of $k$ positive integers there exists $1\leq i\leq u$ such that there are $s$ integers in the interval that are divisible by $p_i$?
